chapters.title: As we get older, we learn about many types of individuals and their characteristics
chapters.text:
As humans, we acquire connections and stereotypes associated with different people from different places; it is a continuous process of bias that is mainly unconscious, unintentional, or unexamined. What occurs is that we come across someone who fits into one of these categories, and then all the associations stored in our memories begin to impact the way we behave. These responses can emerge so swiftly and automatically that we are not even aware of the influence they may be exerting on us. The truth is that these emotions may be at odds with our stated moral principles.

The “All people are different” truism does not imply that some are better than others; it is a mere fact we need to account for when meeting a new person.

This tidbit discusses possible solutions to the effects of the stereotypes and biases we experience. Through these achievable solutions, we may incorporate structured decision-making into our institutions and organizations to limit the influence of bias in our day-to-day operations. For example, when communicating with children and adults, we would learn how to be deliberate about the messages we send and reconsider the media we present to prevent perpetuating negative preconceptions. Furthermore, this summary helps us understand that we may establish new social standards for how we interact with one another, making the process of being biased and prejudiced less regular and natural.

chapters.title: Implicit bias promotes the very thing we fight against
chapters.text:
Implicit bias is unconscious favoritism or prejudicial treatment of a specific race, gender, or social group. In this chapter, we’ll look at how it has shaped racism.

The idea of racism was based on the unfounded belief that white people are innately superior to other races. Prior to empirical evidence, white psychologists and scientists assumed that “colored people,” particularly Blacks, lacked the mental ability to compete or coexist with white people. For example, in 1942, 25,000 white assembly line employees protested working alongside their Black counterparts. Blacks were therefore never considered for major posts, and employers badly mistreated them.

In response to the flawed assumptions made by white people towards Blacks, authors and psychologists like Frederick Douglass and W.E.B Dubois made it plain that the only difference between white people and Blacks was skin color.

The prejudice towards Blacks stems from white people’s urge to feel superior. The apparent evidence of Black brilliance shaped people's perceptions of racism. During the height of the civil rights and anti-racism movements, many white people openly supported Blacks, even marching together to protest tyranny and unequal treatment. But, according to Jessica Nordell, white people’s assistance comes from a position of necessity, not concern. She said people who marched and supported Blacks did so to feel superior. When a white man walked with numerous Black men, he understood he was unlikely to be injured or detained during a riot. But if the risks of being attacked were comparable to those of a Black man, fewer white people would have joined the march.

Consciously or not, it’s almost impossible for a white person to avoid being racist towards a Black person because of how easy it is to ignore stereotypical behavior.

Our surroundings teach us about various groups of individuals, and some parts of this information are accurate while others are not. Discriminatory behavior can create disparities, fueling cultural biases and initiating the cycle of racism and violence. We notice various categories of identity, including race, gender, age, and more, each with connotations that a perceiver might absorb.

The major problem with implicit bias is that not many people are aware of its existence, making it difficult to notice. Racism has become systemic and so well-hidden in society that it’s almost impossible to detect.

chapters.title: Stereotypes play a significant role in how white people view people of other races
chapters.text:
In 2015, Universal Studios came up with a great idea to advertise their upcoming movie Straight Outta Compton. They created targeted Facebook Ads based on skin color. If the Facebook algorithm detected you to be Black, the movie ad you’d see would be that of real-life members of the hip hop group N.W.A., Dr. Dre, and Ice Cube driving around their former neighborhood in a documentary. You'd have heard Dre discuss N.W.A. as a sort of peaceful protest and the group's attempts to inspire the next generation. Then you'd witness artists at work and a white manager discussing raw talent. However, if you were white, the ad would be different. A Black lady wielding a shotgun appears in the first 20 seconds of the ad, followed by lights, sirens, and young Black males at a bar sipping dark liquor and Ice Cube being handcuffed and slammed into a police vehicle.

The scene also showed a shotgun drawn from a duffel bag by Eazy-E, a row of Black guys face down on the sidewalk, and a white manager asking that his clients be released.

According to Universal’s EVP of marketing, Doug Neil, the ad format was responsible for a substantial part of the movie’s financial success. By confirming and showing people’s bias, they created a commercial success.

Stereotypes promote confidence in uncertain situations because it feels more comfortable to validate familiar preconceptions.

Stereotypes and biases have one thing in common: they all entail some kind of prediction. For example, when we listen to music, our thoughts anticipate each note that will follow, and we get pleasure from hearing the pattern we've been anticipating.

Generally, people do not like to be wrong, which is why Universal Studios created a targeted ad based on people's perceptions. Both ads maintained white people’s view of Blacks as violent and ruthless and confirmed the Black person’s perspective of the realities of a fellow Black.

Everyday biases have adverse effects on those at the receiving end. It means people are then judged based on the perception of who they are, which is misleading and can result in varying levels of disasters.

chapters.title: Rushed assumptions make it difficult to be less biased about people
chapters.text:
Professor of Psychology Patricia Devine had one of her undergraduate students propose a program to remove prejudices. As a result, Devine and her team have been working on this project ever since, basing their technique on cognitive behavior therapy. They believe that it's not enough to be conscious of the issue to modify one's behavior; one must also be motivated to try and give clear methods to replace old reactions with new ones. The idea was to halt automatic reactions, so they created a workshop consisting of three categories.

• The first raises people's awareness of unwitting prejudice.
• The second increases their incentive to cease.
• The third offers suggestions for adjustment.

Devine and her colleagues have tried this with students, professors, and businesses. Their research shows that educating individuals about prejudice in this manner may start changing behavior.

Individuals who are mistaken for someone else of their same racial or ethnic background can feel unseen and erased; they may also be wrongfully convicted. ~ Jessica Nordell

When preconceptions emerge, people should actively replace them with other pictures. It would be helpful to look for situational explanations for a person's conduct rather than thinking it stems from an innate trait.

Reach out and get to know individuals who are not like you and learn more about them to give you a better perspective on being less biased and unfair.

Although we are unique individuals, we are historical and interconnected beings. Individuals alone cannot overcome the constraints imposed by policies, laws, and algorithms. In addition, we are emotional creatures that experience complicated feelings; all of these influence how we react to others. In these situations, people may not take the time to consider the viewpoint of others or the context of their actions. In some cases, decisions can be life-or-death, primarily when we have learned to fear one another because, although it is possible to influence or control people’s biases, it’s practically impossible to override them. Stereotypes will always exist, but we can reduce their effects.

chapters.title: The first step to overcoming negative stereotypes is accepting that we’re the only ones capable of changing our thought processes
chapters.text:
As much as we would love the law to protect Black people from racist oppression, especially police brutality, there’s still a long way to go to ensure that Blacks are not killed because of their skin color. The law can only act to prevent or punish a crime; it doesn’t have the power to change people’s stereotypes about Blacks.

In 2019, George Floyd, a harmless Black man wrongly accused of shoplifting, was murdered by Derreck Chauvin, a white police officer. Chauvin had knelt on Floyd’s neck for approximately nine minutes, even after Floyd had reportedly passed out. There have been several reported cases of police brutality in the United States, and a large number of those cases have been between white police officers and Blacks. In a survey conducted by Connie Rice, an American activist and civil rights lawyer, many white police officers admitted they were scared of Black men.

Fear provokes irrational behavior, and it can lead to disasters.

In a separate survey, Rice also discovered that when people reported crimes in Black neighborhoods, the police responded with less urgency. Blacks remain dangerous, volatile, and extremely violent to many officers, especially when confronted. Although this stereotype holds no factual evidence, it has remained in the American police system. As a result, it has become almost impossible for these police officers to be objective or rational since their first instinct is fear.

Humans are naturally biased when identifying and classifying individuals from familiar groups. In segregated societies, we're more familiar with our own race. However, with greater exposure to other races, the issue of racism can decrease significantly. Learning to differentiate between members of various groups improves our ability to recognize people and process sensory information more accurately.

chapters.title: The world needs more inclusion of diversified people to strike a balance in places where oppression exists
chapters.text:
The common phrase “experience is the best teacher” heavily applies to racism and how white people fail to identify the obvious. Social media platforms, especially Twitter, are breeding grounds for online trolls who direct their abuse to people of color. A 2020 analysis by Jessica Nordell found that Black women are 84% more likely to be harassed on Twitter than white women. The reason for this is not far-fetched: homogeneity. The founders of Twitter are all white men, which explains why they didn’t factor in the possibility of the platform being used to target Blacks racially. Since none of the founders had encountered or experienced racism and abuse based on skin color, they had no incentive to make Twitter a safe place for Blacks.

Twitter is good at two things: real time information and abuse. [...] They both are disseminated in the exact same way. ~ Leslie Miley

Homogeneity is responsible for the systemic racism present in workplaces, especially in white-dominated areas. Despite Black and Latino students earning more than 18% of bachelor’s degrees in math, computer science, and electrical engineering, they are significantly underrepresented in the tech industry, making up only 4% of Google’s technical staff. This disparity persists even though many of these students come from leading educational institutions. Additionally, within a span of 12 years, half of the women in STEM fields leave their careers, often due to the impact of discrimination and societal pressures.

As long as Blacks are underrepresented and oppressed, they’ll struggle to find justice.

We must foster an inclusive environment that never judges people for the color of their skin. However, in the process of advocating for an inclusive environment, white people need to pay attention to sensitive issues that affect Blacks. In an unfortunate incident, Mekka Okereke, a Nigerian American engineering leader, was in a meeting where someone mentioned that the company’s outgoing mails should be sent properly and not in a way that looks like a Nigerian sent it. Obviously offended, Mekka diffused the situation by making light of the insult, but she made it clear that she would have preferred if someone else stood up for her. However, since none of her white colleagues understood how insulted she felt, they had no incentive to defend her.

Unless white people learn more about the history of Black people, there’ll always be subtle and blatant systemic racism in the environment.

Did you know? According to a survey carried out by Jessica Nordell, as of 2020, Twitter’s founding tech team is 74.8% male.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
It is possible to end bias when we change our thoughts, emotions, and behaviors. Another option is to alter organizational procedures, structures, and culture. These are inextricably linked: people build the procedures, structures, and corporate culture, which in turn affect individuals' attitudes and behaviors.

Altering one's cognitive habits is seldom a straightforward task and is not a panacea for all ills. Addressing individual bias is not enough to eliminate disparities and societal imbalances, which are the lingering effects of historical exclusion, unequal access, exploitative economic practices, and other unjust systems built on flawed foundations. True justice requires large-scale structural reforms, from redesigning public safety and prison systems to restructuring the entire economy.

The importance of true inner, individual transformation, on the other hand, cannot be overemphasized. Laws and institutions are born out of the emotions, motives, and awareness of human beings. Policies are produced by people, and people interpret, execute, and abide by the policies they make. Although we may demolish buildings or regulations, we are still left with human brains that must invent what will replace them.

This tidbit’s ideas provide a springboard; we can start by noting our biased responses, which are frequently unconscious but may be seen and interrogated. Mindfulness will help us perceive these emotions more clearly and govern our internal landscape so that bias doesn't dominate our replies. We can build meaningful relationships with individuals who are different from us, increasing our understanding of others.

Try this

Avoid judging people based on your perception of them. Ask questions if you are unsure about an issue, read books that keep you informed about stereotypes, and keep up with current trends and news.

Read it till the end?
